sections.markdown:
# DEFINE-CONDITION: Your Own Condition Classes

Specialize your error handling by defining your own condition classes. `define-condition` looks almost exactly like `defclass`, but the metaclass is `condition` instead of `standard-object`:

```lisp
(define-condition CONDITION-NAME (PARENT-CONDITION ...)
  ((slot-name :initarg :slot-name :reader slot-name)
   ...)
  (:report (lambda (condition stream) ...)))
```

Key points:

* The **parent** list determines what handlers catch the condition. For a recoverable error, inherit from `error`. For advisory conditions, `condition` or `warning`.
* **Slots** work like `defclass` slots: `:initarg`, `:reader`, `:initform`.
* The `:report` option is the printer used by the debugger and `princ`. It receives the condition and a stream.

Signal your class with `(error 'my-error :slot1 v1 :slot2 v2)` (or `signal`/`warn` for non-fatal kinds). Handlers that specialise on your class — or any ancestor — will catch it.

sections.code: (progn (define-condition bad-input (error) ((value :initarg :value :reader bad-input-value)) (:report (lambda (c s) (format s "bad input: ~s" (bad-input-value c))))) (handler-case (error 'bad-input :value 42) (bad-input (c) (bad-input-value c))))
sections.explain: We define a condition with one slot, signal it, and pull the slot out in the handler.

sections.code: (progn (define-condition bad-input (error) ((value :initarg :value :reader bad-input-value)) (:report (lambda (c s) (format s "bad input: ~s" (bad-input-value c))))) (handler-case (error 'bad-input :value :oops) (error (c) (princ-to-string c))))
sections.explain: A handler on the generic ERROR class still catches our subclass. PRINC-TO-STRING uses the :REPORT function.
No :REPORT is required — the slot is still accessible through its reader.

sections.prompt: Define a condition OUT-OF-RANGE inheriting from ERROR with slots LOW, HIGH, GOT (all initargs, with matching readers OUT-OF-RANGE-LOW, OUT-OF-RANGE-HIGH, OUT-OF-RANGE-GOT). Then define CHECK-RANGE (N LOW HIGH) that signals the condition when N is outside [LOW, HIGH], otherwise returns N. Catch it in tests with HANDLER-CASE.
sections.starter:
(define-condition out-of-range (error)
  ((low  :initarg :low  :reader out-of-range-low)
   (high :initarg :high :reader out-of-range-high)
   (got  :initarg :got  :reader out-of-range-got)))

(defun check-range (n low high)
  )
sections.hint: (if (<= low n high) n (error 'out-of-range :low low :high high :got n)).
